content.text: platform with native AI capabilities. You build automations by connecting trigger nodes, processing nodes, and AI nodes in a canvas, wiring outputs to inputs. n8n's AI Agent node runs a tool-calling loop against any supported LLM, with the agent's tools being other n8n nodes.

attrs.alt: n8n workflow — Trigger → HTTP Request → AI Agent (with Tool sub-nodes) → Email/Slack output, with Memory Manager and 400+ integrations

content.text:
# Option 1: npx (quickest for trying n8n)
npx n8n

# Option 2: npm global install
npm install -g n8n
n8n start

# Option 3: Docker (recommended for production)
docker run -it --rm \
  -p 5678:5678 \
  -v ~/.n8n:/home/node/.n8n \
  n8nio/n8n

# Access the editor at http://localhost:5678
The editor runs in the browser at
, then drag nodes from the left panel onto the canvas. Connect node outputs to inputs by clicking the output handle and dragging to the next node.
n8n Cloud (cloud.n8n.io) provides a hosted version with no infrastructure to manage. Self-hosted is preferred when you need custom node packages, access to internal networks, or full data control.
a directed graph of nodes; runs when its trigger fires or when manually executed.
a single processing step — trigger, action, AI, or logic. Each node type has a fixed set of parameters configured via the UI.
a template inside a parameter field, wrapped in
, that evaluates against the current item's JSON data.
n8n's data unit — each node processes an array of items. A node can fan-out (one item → many) or reduce (many items → one).
securely stored key-value pairs shared across workflows — API keys, OAuth tokens, database passwords.
Expressions reference data from the current item or from any upstream node by name. They are JavaScript-like but run in a sandboxed evaluator, not Node.js — not all JS APIs are available.
accessor lets you reach any upstream node's output by name, not just the immediately previous node. This is useful when merging data from multiple parallel branches.

node implements a tool-calling loop: the LLM sees the system prompt, the user message (an expression), and a list of available tools. It calls tools in sequence until it produces a final answer. Configure it by:
Setting the Chat Model (OpenAI, Anthropic, Google, Groq, or any OpenAI-compatible endpoint)
Writing the User Message (can be an expression referencing upstream data)
Attaching Tool sub-nodes by connecting nodes to the Agent's Tool input port
Optionally attaching a Memory Manager node
Using HTTP Request as an Agent Tool
Attach an HTTP Request node to the Agent's Tool port to let the agent call any REST API. Use
in parameter fields to declare which values the agent should populate.
generates an argument in the tool schema. The agent reads the description to know what value to supply — write it like a docstring.
When no built-in node matches your tool logic, attach a
to the Agent's Tool port. The Code node receives the arguments the agent passed via its input JSON.
The Code node runs in a sandboxed Node.js environment. You have access to
(environment variables set in n8n settings), and the standard Node.js globals except
for external packages (add those in the n8n Docker image instead).
node to the AI Agent to enable multi-turn conversations. The agent automatically reads and writes conversation history; you set the session identifier (typically a user or session ID).
keeps the last N message pairs verbatim. Simple, predictable token cost.
keeps recent messages verbatim but summarises older ones. Better for long sessions but adds an extra LLM call per summary.
the AI Agent to clean, trim, or restructure upstream data into the shape your prompt expects.
Trimming inputs to 2000 characters before passing to the agent prevents token overuse on bulk processing workflows. The Code node's output becomes the
The AI Agent's User Message field is an expression — you can interpolate upstream data directly into the prompt.

Keep the system prompt static (who the agent is, what format to return) and use the user message expression for dynamic content. This makes it easier to cache system prompt tokens with providers that support prompt caching.
Large workflows become hard to maintain. Break them into
using the Execute Workflow node to call a named workflow as a function.

Sub-workflows run in the same n8n instance and share credentials. The parent workflow can call the sub-workflow synchronously (wait for result) or fire-and-forget. This is the closest n8n equivalent to modular agent composition.
(a separate workflow triggered on failures) in the workflow settings. It receives
with the node name, error message, and stack trace.
Use IF nodes inside the error workflow to dispatch: rate-limit errors → retry queue, auth errors → alert on-call engineer, data errors → send to dead-letter Slack channel.
Comparison: n8n AI Workflows vs Code-Based Agents
Deploy as many replicas as needed
expressions in one node cannot reference nodes that come later in the workflow — only upstream nodes. Reference
only for nodes that run before the current one.
if an upstream node returns 10 items, the AI Agent node runs 10 times — once per item. Use a
node to combine items first if you want one agent call over all data.
n8n passes the full item JSON to the agent. Trim irrelevant fields in a Code node before the Agent to avoid expensive, slow calls.
which might collide). Two workflows sharing the same session ID read each other's history.
credentials are per-user in n8n Cloud, shared across the instance in self-hosted. In team environments, restrict credential access by role to avoid accidental exposure.
For bulk tasks (classifying 1000 support tickets, summarising daily news articles) use the
node to group items, run the Agent on each batch, then
results before writing to a destination.

Processing items in batches of 10–20 reduces the number of LLM calls compared to per-item agent runs, and keeps each prompt within a predictable token budget. Ask the model to return structured JSON (array) for easier aggregation.

If the batch prompt returns malformed JSON, the downstream parsing fails for all items in that batch. Add a Code node after the Agent to parse and validate — fall back to per-item classification for batches where parsing fails.
RAG Workflows with Vector Store Nodes

n8n's Vector Store nodes (v1.29+) support Retrieval-Augmented Generation: ingest documents into an embedding store once, then attach a Vector Store Tool to the AI Agent so it can search the knowledge base per conversation.
Supported vector stores include Pinecone, Qdrant, Weaviate, PGVector, and an in-memory option for testing. The
parameter controls how many chunks the agent retrieves per tool call — 3–5 is typical.
By default n8n runs workflows in-process.
offloads execution to worker processes via a Redis queue, letting you scale horizontally with multiple workers while the main instance handles only the editor and trigger webhooks.

# n8n Queue Mode — scale workers horizontally
# Requires Redis for the job queue

# docker-compose.yml excerpt
services:
  n8n-main:
    image: n8nio/n8n
    environment:
      - EXECUTIONS_MODE=queue
      - QUEUE_BULL_REDIS_HOST=redis
      - N8N_ENCRYPTION_KEY=your-key
    ports:
      - "5678:5678"
    depends_on:
      - redis

  n8n-worker:
    image: n8nio/n8n
    command: n8n worker
    environment:
      - EXECUTIONS_MODE=queue
      - QUEUE_BULL_REDIS_HOST=redis
      - N8N_ENCRYPTION_KEY=your-key  # must match main
    deploy:
      replicas: 4   # spin up 4 workers
    depends_on:
      - redis

  redis:
    image: redis:7-alpine
All workers and the main instance must share the same
to decrypt credentials. In production, store it in a secrets manager and inject it at runtime via the environment.
per worker to cap parallel executions and prevent out-of-memory crashes.
in Queue Mode, webhooks are received by the main instance and queued to workers. Ensure the main instance is reachable from the internet; workers do not need public exposure.
node (pause mid-execution for a callback), sticky sessions must route resumed executions to the same worker or be disabled in favour of external state storage.
n8n is a visual workflow automation platform with a native AI Agent node that runs a tool-calling loop against any supported LLM.
Tools are other n8n nodes (HTTP Request, Code, built-in integrations) attached to the Agent's Tool input port.
The Memory Manager node enables multi-turn conversation with Window Buffer or Summary Buffer strategies.
Use Code nodes to pre-process data before the Agent and to implement custom tool logic not covered by built-in nodes.
Sub-workflows (Execute Workflow node) decompose large automations into reusable, testable units.
Error Workflows catch failures and route to alerting, retry queues, or dead-letter channels based on error type.
